verses.text: Agur confesses his ignorance and the surpassing greatness of God: 'Who hath ascended up into heaven, or descended?... what is his name, and what is his son's name, if thou canst tell?' Wisdom begins in humility. The Creator's majesty is beyond our finding out.

Agur confesses his ignorance and the surpassing greatness of God: 'Who hath ascended up into heaven, or descended?... what is his name, and what is his son's name, if thou canst tell?' Wisdom begins in humility. The Creator's majesty is beyond our finding out.

Agur confesses his ignorance and the surpassing greatness of God: 'Who hath ascended up into heaven, or descended?... what is his name, and what is his son's name, if thou canst tell?' Wisdom begins in humility. The Creator's majesty is beyond our finding out.

'Every word of God is pure: he is a shield unto them that put their trust in him'; add not to His words, lest He reprove you and you be found a liar. Scripture is flawless. To tamper with God's word is to invite His rebuke.

'Every word of God is pure: he is a shield unto them that put their trust in him'; add not to His words, lest He reprove you and you be found a liar. Scripture is flawless. To tamper with God's word is to invite His rebuke.

Agur asks two things: remove vanity and lies far from him, and give him neither poverty nor riches but 'food convenient for me,' lest he be full and deny God, or poor and steal. He prays for contentment. Both excess and want carry their own temptations.

Accuse not a servant to his master; there is a generation that curses parents, is pure in its own eyes yet unwashed, proud-eyed, and devours the poor with its teeth. Agur names corrupt generations. Self-righteous pride and cruelty to the poor mark a godless age.

The leech's two daughters cry, 'Give, give'; four things are never satisfied — the grave, the barren womb, the dry earth, and the fire; the eye that mocks a father shall be plucked out. Greed is insatiable. Contempt for parents meets a terrible end.

Four things are too wonderful for Agur — the eagle in the air, the serpent on the rock, the ship in the sea, and 'the way of a man with a maid'; so too the adulteress wipes her mouth and says she has done no wrong. Some things amaze us. Sin hardened denies its own guilt.

For four things the earth is disquieted — a servant made king, a fool filled with food, an odious woman married, and a maid who supplants her mistress. Disorder unsettles the world. Those lifted above their place often misuse it.

For four things the earth is disquieted — a servant made king, a fool filled with food, an odious woman married, and a maid who supplants her mistress. Disorder unsettles the world. Those lifted above their place often misuse it.

Four things are little but exceeding wise — the ants storing food, the conies sheltering in rocks, the locusts marching in ranks though kingless, and the spider that dwells in kings' palaces. Wisdom shines in small creatures. Diligence and order outweigh mere size or strength.

Four things move with stately grace — the lion that turns from none, the greyhound, the he-goat, and a king against whom none rises. Dignity has its bearing. There is a fitting majesty in strength rightly held.

If you have done foolishly in lifting yourself up, or have thought evil, 'lay thine hand upon thy mouth'; as churning brings butter, so forcing wrath brings strife. Restrain pride and anger. Pressing a quarrel only squeezes out conflict.
